coredata.dc:title: Full color emitting fluorescent carbon material as reversible pH sensor with multicolor live cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Vinay
Kaur, Navpreet
Tiwari, Pranav
Mobin, Shaikh M.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nano materials are developed as a cytocompatible alternative to semiconducting quantum dots for bioimaging and fluorescence-based sensing. The green alternatives for the synthesis of carbon materials are imminent. The present study demonstrates microwave based one step quick synthesis of fluorescent carbon material (FCM) having three variants: (i) un-doped fluorescent carbon material (UFCM) (ii) nitrogen doped FCM (N@FCM), and (iii) nitrogen & phosphorus co-doped FCM (N-P@FCM) using sugarcane extract as a carbon source. The N doping was performed using ethylenediamine and phosphoric acid was used for P doping. The heteroatom doped FCM were synthesized due to insolubility of UFCM in water. Unlike, UFCM, the N@FCM and N-P@FCM were found to be highly soluble in water. The N-P@FCM shows highest quantum yield among the three. The N-P@FCM was explored for alkaline pH sensing and it shows a quenching of fluorescence in the pH range 09–14. The sensing behaviour shows reversibility and high selectivity. Further, the sensor was also investigated for their biocompatibility and hence employed as a promising multicolour probe for cancer cell imaging. The generality in cell imaging was investigated by flow cytometry. The hetero-atom doped green carbon-dots may open new avenues for sensing and selective cellular targeting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dots
Bioimaging
Sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and Optical Characterization of FCM
Cell Viability, Cellular Uptake and Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural and Optical characterization of FCM. (a–d) UFCM: PXRD, TEM, UV–Vis and Fluorescence spectra respectively. (e–l) PXRD, TEM, UV–Vis and fluorescence of N@FCM and N-P@FCM. (m–o) XPS survey spectrum of FCM.
Relative fluorescence intensities of FCM (a–c) at different UV exposure time and (d–f) varied temperature (−80 °C) to (+60 °C).
The fluorescence-based sensing response of N-P@FCM at different pH in the pH range 9–14.
The selectivity in the sensing response of N-P@FCM at different pH with different interfering species.
Confocal microscopic analysis of multicolor intracellular fluorescence of N-P@FCM in various cancer cells.
The flow cytometry studies for cellular uptake in MCF-7 cell line.
Synthesis of un-doped and hetero-atom doped fluorescent carbon material (FCM).
Performance comparison with reported pH sensors.

head.ce:title: Full color emitting fluorescent carbon material as reversible pH sensor with multicolor live cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Discipline of Biosciences and Bio-Medical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore 453552, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Discipline of Biosciences and Bio-Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Discipline of Metallurgy Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore 453552, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Discipline of Metallurgy Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Discipline of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore 453552, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Discipline of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Indore, Simrol, Khandwa Road, Indore 453552, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nano materials are developed as a cytocompatible alternative to semiconducting quantum dots for bioimaging and fluorescence-based sensing. The green alternatives for the synthesis of carbon materials are imminent. The present study demonstrates microwave based one step quick synthesis of fluorescent carbon material (FCM) having three variants: (i) un-doped fluorescent carbon material (UFCM) (ii) nitrogen doped FCM (N@FCM), and (iii) nitrogen & phosphorus co-doped FCM (N-P@FCM) using sugarcane extract as a carbon source. The N doping was performed using ethylenediamine and phosphoric acid was used for P doping. The heteroatom doped FCM were synthesized due to insolubility of UFCM in water. Unlike, UFCM, the N@FCM and N-P@FCM were found to be highly soluble in water. The N-P@FCM shows highest quantum yield among the three. The N-P@FCM was explored for alkaline pH sensing and it shows a quenching of fluorescence in the pH range 09–14. The sensing behaviour shows reversibility and high selectivity. Further, the sensor was also investigated for their biocompatibility and hence employed as a promising multicolour probe for cancer cell imaging. The generality in cell imaging was investigated by flow cytometry. The hetero-atom doped green carbon-dots may open new avenues for sensing and selective cellular targeting.

This work demonstrates a one-step, quick and green synthesis of fluorescent carbon materials for reversible pH sensing in the range 9–14 along with interesting full color emission for intracellular employability.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: The benign synthesis process by employing biomolecules and plant tissues are being explored for the synthesis of nanomaterials and are shown to have added advantages over chemically synthesized materials [1]. Ever since the inception of semiconducting quantum dots for bioimaging applications, their higher toxicity remains a concern [2], while poor chemical stability and photo-bleaching limits the use of conventional organic fluorophores. The carbon-based nanomaterials have emerged as a promising fluorescent alternative to unstable organic dyes and toxic semiconducting quantum dots [3]. There are several methods reported so far for the synthesis of carbon dots such as electrochemical [4], microwave [5], hydrothermal [6,7], arc discharge [8], laser ablation [9] and refluxing [10]. However, due to the use of harsh chemicals, tedious reaction conditions and time-consuming synthesis, there is a compelling requirement for an eco-friendly, quick and economical route for synthesis of fluorescent carbon material with large-scale synthesis capability [11–14]. The eco-friendly synthesis of carbon nanodots using abundant carbohydrates and proteins present in different biomass are recently explored and is an emerging field [15–20]. The quantum yield is an important parameter to assess the quality of a fluorophore and efforts have been made to increase quantum yield and tune the emission wavelengths using heteroatom doping [21–25].

The pH sensors have found numerous applications in the fields ranging from environmental science to medical diagnosis and are an essential part of biological research. The electrochemical pH sensor is well established but suffers from disadvantages like the requirement of frequent calibrations, electrode corrosion and electrical interference [26]. On the other hand, recently growing fluorescence-based pH sensors offer advantage of high sensitivity, ease of miniaturization and contactless measurement [27,28].
The bioimaging using carbon dots is an exciting and growing area [29–32]. The lipophilicity of cell staining agent is of the utmost importance that can derive these materials into the cells.

Herein, we report a one-step benign synthesis of fluorescent carbon material (FCM) using sugarcane extract as a precursor. The hetero-atom doped variant N@FCM and N-P@FCM were also synthesized. The N-P@FCM was employed as a pH-responsive material with the added advantage of promising bio-imaging application along with excellent cytocompatibility.

The sugarcane was procured from local market. Phosphoric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Different metal salts of analytical grade were procured from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck and were used without further purification.

Anton-Paar, Monowave 300 Microwave reactor was used for synthesis. The absorption studies were performed on a Varian Cary 100 Bio UV–Visible spectrophotometer. A Fluoromax spectrofluorometer was used for fluorescence spectrophotometric studies. The powder X-ray diffraction was recorded using Rigaku SmartLab X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (1.54 Å). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on PHI 5000 Versa Prob II system. FT-IR was recorded on a Bio-Rad FTS 3000MX instrument. TEM was performed on Philips CM-200 transmission electron microscope. Confocal microscopy was performed using an Olympus confocal laser scanning microscope. Flow cytometry was performed on a BD LSRFortessa.

A one-step microwave assisted pyrolysis was used for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon material (FCM). Sugarcane extract was collected by grinding sugarcane. 10 mL of sugarcane extract was subjected to microwave treatment at 150 °C for 30 min. The black powder, undoped FCM (UFCM) was collected and air dried. For the synthesis of nitrogen-doped FCM (N@FCM), 350 μL of ethylenediamine was added to sugarcane extract and subjected to microwave irradiation. For the synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped FCM (N-P@FCM), 350 μL of ethylenediamine and 350 μL of phosphoric acid was added to the sugarcane extract before microwave irradiation.

The sensing of pH was performed at room temperature. The N-P@FCM was suspended in water with a concentration of 10 μg/mL and the same was used for all sensing experiments. The fluorescence spectra were recorded within 2 min of the addition of N-P@FCM in the solution of different pH.

For bioimaging studies, various cell lines i.e. prostate cancer (DU145), breast cancer (MCF-7) and skin melanoma (A375) cells were seeded in 12 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 under a humidified environment. Further, the cells were treated with N-P@FCM and incubated for 4 h and subjected to confocal microscopic imaging using sequential laser scan of 405 nm, 488 nm, 515 nm and 559 nm on an Olympus confocal microscope.

The flow cytometry studies were conducted using λex.- 488 nm. The cells were seeded in 6 well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 under humidified environment. One sample was kept unstained while another was treated with N-P@FCM similar to confocal experiments.

A series of fluorescent carbon material (FCM) was synthesized by a single step, benign approach using sugarcane extract as a carbon source under microwave irradiation. The FCM consists of: (i) Undoped/As synthesized FCM (UFCM), (ii) Nitrogen-doped FCM (N@FCM) using ethylenediamine, and (iii) Nitrogen and Phosphorus co-doped FCM (N-P@FCM) using ethylenediamine and phosphoric acid (Scheme 1 ). The challenge with UFCM was its solubility in water and hence water-soluble alternatives N@FCM and N-P@FCM were sought. The FCM was extensively characterized for its structural and optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and Optical Characterization of FCM
ce:section.ce:para: The typical XRD profile of UFCM exhibited in Fig. 1(a) shows a broad peak which is centered at 21.15°. The corresponding interlayer spacing was determined to be 0.41 nm which is larger than the lattice spacing of graphene [002] plane [33]. The increase in lattice spacing indicates an increase in amorphous nature which may be attributed to the presence of more oxygen-containing groups in UFCM [34]. The TEM micrographs shown in Fig. 1(b) exhibit synthesis of irregular-shaped nanoparticles, which are amorphous in nature indicated by the SAED pattern in the inset of Fig. 1(b). The optical characteristics of UFCM were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectrum of UFCM (Fig. 1(c)) shows a sharp absorption peak centered at 281 nm which was previously attributed to the π–π* transitions of conjugated CC bonds and n–π* of CO bonds [35]. The fluorescence spectrum of UFCM represents maximum emission at 440 nm. Interestingly, the UFCM shows a wavelength tuned emission behaviour as shown in Fig. 1(d). The increase in excitation wavelength results in a very evident bathochromic shift of emission wavelength. The UFCM was also tested for its thermal stability up to 1000 °C using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) at a scan rate of 5 °C/min under N2 atmosphere (Fig. S1(a)). The UFCM exhibited continuous weight loss with no distinct weight loss events. A total of 34.51% residual weight was observed up to 1000 °C. The initial weight loss up to 200 °C is due to the loss of physically adsorbed water molecule. A relatively steep decomposition near 350 °C may be due to the loss of CO2. The weight loss above 450 °C is mainly due to the pyrolytic decomposition of the carbonaceous material.

Further, the heteroatom doped water soluble N@FCM and N-P@FCM were characterized. The powder XRD profile of N@FCM and N-P@FCM shows broad peaks centered at 21.8° and 23.35° respectively. The corresponding interlayer spacing is determined to be 0.38 nm and 0.40 nm (Fig. 1(e–f)). The TEM micrographs of N@FCM and N-P@FCM shows well dispersed nearly spherical particles having an average particle size of 10.2 and 10.7 nm, respectively. The absence of lattice fringes ascertains the amorphous nature of N@FCM and N-P@FCM (Fig. 1(g–h), Fig. S2). The UV–Visible absorption spectrum of N@FCM represents a slight absorption at 281 nm and shows an excitation-dependent emission as exhibited in Fig. 1(i–j). On the other hand, N-P@FCM shows absorption band around 330 nm (Fig. 1(k)) with an emission maximum at 445 nm. The fluorescence spectra of N-P@FCM excited at varying wavelength is depicted in Fig. 1(l) which exhibits a similar excitation-dependent emission. The strong wavelength tuned emission in N@FCM and N-P@FCM can be attributed to the size variation and distinctive emission traps at their surface [36]. The thermal stability of N@FCM and N-P@FCM is shown in Fig. S1(b–c). The N@FCM shows two-step degradation, 50% weight loss was observed up to 250 °C. The gradual weight loss is due to the degradation of surface functional groups. Almost complete decomposition of N@FCM occurs up to 650 °C, while the N-P@FCM retained 20% of the initial weight up to 800 °C. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) of N@FCM and N-P@FCM (Fig. S3) exhibit peaks of CO stretching vibrations (~1640 cm−1) (Zhao et al., 2015), bending vibrations of CN (1112 cm−1) [37], OH stretching vibration (~3430 cm−1), CH (~2920 cm−1), CN stretching (~1050 cm−1) [38] and C-H/N-H (1320–1440 cm−1) [39]. The new peak at 1162 cm−1 in N-P@FCM can be attributed to the vibrations of PO [40], which indicate successful doping of P atom in N-P@FCM, which was further confirmed by x-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS).

The XPS survey spectrum of UFCM shows C 1s and O 1s signal at 284.6 and 531.9 eV, respectively, while N@FCM shows signal corresponding to C 1s, O 1s and N 1s at 285.5, 531.9 and 399.8 eV (Fig. 1(m–n)). The composition analysis of UFCM through XPS shows the presence of 76.5% carbon and 23.5% oxygen. The XPS survey spectra of N-P@FCM shows the presence of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, P 2s and P 2p at 284.9, 532.0, 400.1, 189.7 and 132.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 1(o)). The elemental composition of N@FCM consists of 63.3% carbon, 7.6% nitrogen and 29.0% oxygen, while the composition analysis of N-P@FCM shows the presence of 67.0% carbon, 7.8% nitrogen, 2.4% phosphorus and 22.8% oxygen. These results further confirm the successful doping of N atom in N@FCM, and co-doping of N and P atoms in N-P@FCM. The high resolution C 1 s spectrum of N@FCM was de-convoluted in three peaks at 284.6 eV (CC), 285.8 eV (CC/CN) and 287.5 eV (CO) while the C 1 s spectra of N-P@FCM shows four peaks at 284.6 eV (CC), 285.8 eV (CC/CN) and 287.6 eV (CO) and 288.8 (OCO) (Fig. S4 (a, c)). The N 1 s spectrum of N@FCM and N-P@FCM was well-fitted into peaks at 399.4 eV (NH), 401.3 eV (C-N/N-N) and 399.5 (NH), 401.1 eV (CN/NN/NP) respectively (Fig. S4(b, d)). The P 2p XPS spectrum of N-P@FCM shows two peaks at 132.5 eV and 133.2 eV which can be assigned to the presence of PC and PO respectively (Fig. S4(e)), which further suggest successful doping of N and P atoms in N-P@FCM.

The fluorescence properties of carbon-based nanomaterial are dependent upon the precursor used and the synthesis methodology adopted. The quantum yield of UFCM, N@FCM and N-P@FCM was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference and determined to be 7.39%, 9.41% and 10.86% respectively. The quantum yield of FCM was found to be relatively higher than some other biosynthesized carbon dots using Aloe vera [41], coriander leaves [42], egg white and yolk [43] and urine [44].

In order to investigate the stability of FCM at harsh conditions, their relative fluorescence intensity was studied at varied UV illumination and temperature. To evaluate the photobleaching possibilities of FCM, the effect of continuous UV illumination using a lamp of 365 nm for a period of 2 h was studied. As evident by Fig. 2 (a–c), the continuous UV-illumination affected the fluorescence response of FCM, with N-P@FCM being maximumly photostable. The UV exposure resulted in approx. 35% and 40% decay in initial fluorescence intensity for UFCM and N@FCM respectively, while N-P@FCM shows only 23% decay. The FCM was found almost stable in the temperature range (−80 °C) to (+60 °C) where more than 85% of maximum intensity was retained ( Fig. 2(d–f)).

Considering the relatively high fluorescence quantum yield and high photostability, N-P@FCM was chosen for sensing applications. The pH sensing behaviour of N-P@FCM was investigated at different pH values. The fluorescence intensity does not change much in the pH range 1 to 8.5 (Fig. S5), while a significant fluorescence quenching is observed in the pH range 9–14. The fluorescence titration of N-P@FCM with increasing pH shows a consistent decay in fluorescence intensity as depicted in Fig. 3(a). The fluorescence intensity was decreased to almost 9% of the initial intensity at a pH of 14 (Fig. 3(b)). A gradient color change could be observed under UV lamp of 365 nm as the pH increases from 9 to 14. (Inset Fig. 3(b)). The absorption spectra of N-P@FCM do not show much change in absorption band in pH range 9–14 (Fig. S6). The decrease in fluorescence intensity in the basic pH can be attributed to the diminished radiative recombination of electrons in conduction band and holes in valence band, leading to gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity [45].

The fluorescence lifetime is an important parameter to assess the quality of the fluorophore, the fluorescence lifetime of N-P@FCM was investigated in the pH range 09–14. The data ware fitted to a three-exponential decay function. The average FL lifetime of N-P@FCM was reduced from 2.27 ns at pH 9 to 0.99 ns at pH 14 (Table S1, Fig. 3(c)). In order to investigate the repeatability of the sensor, the pH of a working solution of N-P@FCM was repeatedly switched between pH 9 and pH 13 using H2SO4 and NaOH and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at each cycle. As exhibited in Fig. 3(d), the fluorescence of N-P@FCM shows good reversibility between pH 9 and pH 13.

Moreover, the effect of interfering species on the fluorescence intensity of N-P@FCM at different pH was investigated. The presence of twenty different metal ions, anions, biothiols and biomolecules were used as interfering agents. The result shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-P@FCM was not much affected by other competitive interfering agents at the designated pH range (Fig. 4 ). The N-P@FCM has exhibited excellent fluorescence stability in the presence of interfering agents and hence can be used as a selective sensor of pH in a real-life scenario.

Table 1 compares the performance of pH sensing response of N-P@FCM with other recently reported fluorescence and FRET-based pH sensors and shows the superiority of N-P@FCM in terms of broad linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell Viability, Cellular Uptake and Bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the potential of N-P@FCM as a bioimaging agent, the cytotoxicity was first evaluated on prostate cancer cell line DU145 using MTT assay [53]. The results indicate that the N-P@FCM does not much affect the cell viability at a high concentration of 500 μg/mL with an incubation time of 24 h, where more than 70% cells remained live (Fig. S7). The good cytocompatibility and fluorescence efficiency of N-P@FCM make it an ideal candidate for bioimaging applications.

Further, to examine the bioimaging capability of N-P@FCM, prostate cancer cell line DU145 and skin melanoma A375 and cervical cancer cell line HeLa, were incubated with N-P@FCM for 4 h and imaged under confocal laser scanning microscope. The multicolour emission behaviour of N-P@FCM could easily be visualized in the intracellular environment, where an excitation-dependent emission was seen as N-P@FCM emits in blue, green, yellow and red wavelengths covering the entire visible range (Fig. 5 ). The intrinsic full-color emission of N-P@FCM makes them an ideal candidate for intracellular applications and to construct a sensing system by integrating them with various other objects [54].

The cellular uptake of N-P@FCM was also authenticated using flow cytometry on DU145 and MCF-7 cell lines. Due to availability constraints and to benefit from excitation-dependent emission of N-P@FCM, a laser of wavelength 488 nm was used to excite N-P@FCM inside the cells. The unstained/control and N-P@FCM treated cells were incubated for 2–4 h. Further, the cells were trypsinized and a single cell suspension was studied under flow cytometer to compare the fluorescence intensities. A clear shift in the fluorescence intensity of the unstained and treated sample suggests the generality of cell staining using N-P@FCM, since the fluorescence was distributed to the entire population (Fig. 6 and Fig. S8).

In conclusion, the green carbon precursors can be used as a source for low cost, high yield c-dots. The heteroatom doping results in enhanced fluorescence properties and the inherent non-toxicity of biomass could assist excellent biological applicability of c-dots.

In summary, we demonstrate a green synthesis of fluorescent carbon material (FCM) using sugarcane as the sole carbon precursor. The doping of nitrogen and phosphorus (N@FCM and N-P@FCM) offers better solubility, high quantum yield and stability. Interestingly, the N-P@FCM possesses selective pH-dependent fluorescence behaviour with excellent reversibility leading to a potential applicability of N-P@FCM as an On-Off switch. The N-P@FCM also possess excellent multicolour bioimaging property showing emission in full visible range.

Overall, this work provides an eco-friendly route for preparing a stable, selective, reversible pH sensor which is cytocompatible and has an added advantage of multicolour bioimaging, which may lead to potential applicability in the complicated intracellular environment.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Sophisticated Instrumentation Centre, IIT Indore for all the characterization facilities. V. S. and N. K. gratefully thanks UGC, New Delhi for research fellowship. P. T. thanks MHRD for research fellowship. SMM thanks SERB-DST, Govt. of India for a research grant. We sincerely acknowledge ACMS, IIT Kanpur for providing XPS facility. We are grateful to Prof. G. Hundal and Dr. Sanyog Sharma, Gurunanak Dev University for helping in TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2018.04.006.
